source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva
revision_note: Newly added with 8 verified chunks under public domain (2026-07-23).

content.text: The Arhat Parsva was born in Varanasi as the son of King Asvasena and Queen Vama, living 250 years before the nirvana of Mahavira as a prince of noble character.
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

content.text: Parsva renounced his royal life to establish the four great vows (Chaturyama Dharma): non-violence (ahimsa), truthfulness (satya), non-stealing (asteya), and non-possession (aparigraha).
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

content.text: While Parsva sat in deep meditation, the demon Kamatha (his enemy from past lives) raised a terrifying storm, hurling rain, winds, and stones to break his spiritual resolve.
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

content.text: The serpent king Dharanendra rose from the earth and spread his seven hoods over Parsva's head like an umbrella, protecting him from the fury of the storm.
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

content.text: Throughout the storm and the protection, Parsva remained completely detached, feeling neither anger towards the demon nor attachment to the protector, maintaining perfect equanimity.
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

content.text: Parsva taught that all souls are equal and that the highest spiritual duty is the absolute avoidance of harm to any living being, visible or invisible, establishing the foundation of Jain ethics.
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

content.text: After seventy years of teaching, Parsva achieved ultimate liberation (Nirvana) on Mount Sammed Shikhar, which remains today the most sacred pilgrimage site for Jains.
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva

content.text: This entry compiles the translation from Hermann Jacobi's "Jaina Sutras, Part I" (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22, 1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva, which is in the public domain.
content.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Parsva
